title: Source-available licenses are not open source
description: BSL, SSPL, and Elastic-style licenses ship source but restrict use, so they fail OSI criteria and break OSS assumptions.
body: Use when a dependency relicenses or a new dependency declares an unfamiliar license. Source-available licenses (Business Source License, SSPL, Elastic License 2.0) publish source code but add field-of-use or competition restrictions, so they are not OSI-approved open source and an OSS allowlist must not silently accept them. Projects increasingly relicense from Apache/MPL to these between versions. Do not treat 'we can see the source' as equivalent to 'we may use it freely'. Actions: detect these identifiers in the inventory, halt automatic acceptance, and review the field-of-use clause against your usage. Validation signals include the policy gate failing on a source-available identifier. Risk boundary: any source-available license requires explicit human approval before use, since the restriction may forbid your specific deployment.
